Course Title: AI in Public Service Delivery: Reliability Risks
Category: Education & Public Sector: Education, Government Services, Non-Profit organizations
Description: When automated decisions impact citizens directly.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with how public services operate, along with a general curiosity about technology and its role in government or community organisations, is all you need to get started.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key reliability risks that arise when AI systems are used to make or support decisions in public services, and explain how these risks can directly affect citizens who depend on government and non-profit organisations. You will also be able to apply practical frameworks to recognise when automated decision-making may produce unfair, inaccurate, or harmful outcomes, helping you to advocate for more responsible and transparent use of AI in your organisation.

lessons.lesson title: AI in Public Service Delivery: Reliability Risks
lessons.Description: When public services rely on AI systems to make or support decisions, the consequences of those systems failing can be significant for the people who depend on them. This lesson introduces the key reliability risks that arise when AI is used in public sector contexts, from benefit assessments to healthcare triage and beyond. You will explore what can go wrong when automated systems produce errors, behave inconsistently, or are applied in situations they were not designed to handle. The lesson is aimed at those with little or no prior technical knowledge, and focuses on practical understanding rather than complex theory. By the end, you will have a clearer picture of why reliability matters so much when AI decisions directly affect citizens' lives and access to services.
lessons.content: When automated decisions impact citizens directly.

slides.subtitle: From automation to accountability in government.
slides.slidecontent:
• AI handles real government tasks
• Benefits, welfare, licensing, permits
• Decisions affect real people
• Errors carry serious consequences
• Accountability gaps are growing

slides.subtitle: Why accuracy matters in public services.
slides.slidecontent:
• Citizens depend on services working correctly every time
• Vulnerable populations most at risk from errors
• Public trust requires consistent performance
• Stakes higher than private sector applications
• No room for 'good enough' when basic needs are involved

slides.subtitle: When algorithms get it wrong.
slides.slidecontent:
• Benefits wrongly denied due to misread data
• Families left without income during lengthy appeals
• Healthcare triage errors delay critical treatment
• Housing applications rejected on faulty assessments
• Compounding errors when no human oversight exists

slides.subtitle: Challenging decisions made by machines.
slides.slidecontent:
• Appeals processes designed for human decision-makers
• Citizens confused about who or what to challenge
• Corrections can take weeks or months to process
• Lack of transparency in automated reasoning
• Staff unable to explain algorithmic decisions

slides.subtitle: When AI systems reflect unfair patterns.
slides.slidecontent:
• Historical data contains embedded biases
• Certain groups systematically treated unfairly
• Algorithmic discrimination harder to detect than human bias
• Automated systems can amplify existing inequalities
• Legal and ethical compliance challenges

slides.subtitle: The erosion of public confidence.
slides.slidecontent:
• Citizens unaware machines made decisions affecting them
• Black box algorithms resist explanation
• Trust lost faster than it can be rebuilt
• Democratic accountability requires explainable decisions
• Public services must maintain legitimacy

slides.subtitle: Practical steps to manage AI failures.
slides.slidecontent:
• Always keep a human in the loop for high-risk decisions
• Test systems against real-world edge cases regularly
• Document failures and establish clear escalation paths
• Communicate openly when decisions are under review
• Start small, pilot carefully, then scale gradually

slides.subtitle: Core lessons for responsible AI use.
slides.slidecontent:
• AI fails, plan ahead
• Check outputs regularly
• Citizens deserve reliable services
• Errors cause real harm
• Always keep humans involved
• Review, improve, repeat

scripts.script: Welcome to this course on AI in public service delivery, and specifically the reliability risks that come with it. Artificial intelligence is no longer just a concept for the future. It is already being used right now by governments and public bodies to handle real, consequential tasks. We are talking about things like processing benefit claims, assessing welfare eligibility, issuing licences, and managing permits. These are not abstract functions. They directly affect people's lives, their income, their housing, their ability to work. So when something goes wrong, the consequences can be serious. A wrong decision is not just an inconvenience. It can mean someone loses financial support they desperately need. And here is where things get complicated. As AI takes on more of these decisions, it is becoming increasingly unclear who is actually responsible when errors occur. That accountability gap is growing, and it is something we really need to understand.

Public services are different from private companies in a fundamental way: citizens have no choice but to rely on them. When a commercial app makes a mistake, you might use a competitor. But when the benefits system or healthcare service gets something wrong, there is nowhere else to go. This creates an enormous responsibility. People expect these services to work correctly every single time, and that expectation is entirely reasonable. The stakes are particularly high because the people most likely to be affected by errors are often those who are already vulnerable - those who depend most heavily on public support. Unlike private sector applications where failure might mean lost convenience or revenue, here it means real people going without essential support they need to survive.

Let me give you some concrete examples of what happens when these systems fail. A benefits algorithm misreads a data field and suddenly someone's income support is stopped without warning. A healthcare triage system miscalculates risk scores and delays treatment for someone who actually needs urgent care. A housing assessment tool makes a faulty calculation and a family's application is rejected. These aren't hypothetical scenarios - they are real cases that have happened. And here is what makes it worse: when there is no human oversight built into the process, errors do not just sit there quietly. One wrong decision can trigger another, creating a cascade of problems that become increasingly difficult to unravel.

One of the biggest problems citizens face is that most appeals processes were designed with human decision-makers in mind. When someone wants to challenge a decision, they expect to be able to speak to the person who made it, or at least understand their reasoning. But with automated systems, there often is no clear person to appeal to, and the reasoning may be completely opaque. Citizens find themselves trying to challenge decisions made by algorithms they cannot understand, through processes that were never designed for this purpose. Even when corrections are possible, they can take weeks or months to work through official channels, leaving people in limbo. Staff members themselves often cannot explain how an algorithmic decision was reached, making the whole experience deeply frustrating for everyone involved.

AI systems learn from historical data, and that data often reflects the biases and inequalities that have existed in public services for decades. When these patterns are baked into an algorithm, they can become systematically unfair in ways that are much harder to spot than human bias. For example, if past housing allocations favoured certain postcodes, an AI system might continue that pattern automatically. If welfare assessments historically treated some groups more harshly, the algorithm might perpetuate that discrimination. The problem is that algorithmic discrimination can be subtle and statistical, affecting thousands of cases in ways that only become apparent when someone takes a step back and looks at the broader patterns. This raises serious questions about legal compliance and fairness.

Trust is the foundation of effective public services, and it is remarkably fragile. When citizens discover that machines have been making important decisions about their lives without their knowledge, trust begins to erode. When those decisions cannot be properly explained or justified, the erosion accelerates. Democratic societies depend on the idea that public decisions can be scrutinized and held accountable. If algorithms are making choices that affect citizens but cannot be meaningfully challenged or understood, we have a problem that goes beyond technical reliability. It becomes a question of democratic legitimacy. The challenge is that trust, once lost, is incredibly difficult to rebuild, and public services cannot function effectively without it.

So, knowing the risks is one thing, but what do we actually do about them? A few practical principles can make a real difference. First, for any decision that significantly affects someone's life, keep a human involved. Don't let the system have the final word on its own. Second, test your AI regularly, and not just in comfortable, ideal conditions. Throw real-world messy scenarios at it and see how it copes. When things do go wrong, and they will, write it down. Documenting failures helps you spot patterns and improve over time. Make sure your staff know exactly who to call and what to do when something breaks down. And if a citizen's case is being reviewed or queried, tell them. Transparency builds trust. Finally, resist the urge to roll everything out at once. Start with a small pilot, learn from it, and then scale up gradually.

So let's bring everything together. The most important thing to hold onto from this lesson is that AI is not infallible. It will make mistakes, so you need to have a plan in place before that happens, not after. That means checking what the system produces on a regular basis, because errors can creep in quietly over time. And remember who is on the receiving end of these services. These are real people relying on public services to work properly, and when things go wrong, the consequences are not just technical, they are human. That is why keeping people involved in the process is so essential. Humans need to remain in the loop, making judgements, catching problems, and taking responsibility. Finally, this is not a one-off exercise. Reviewing how your AI tools are performing and making improvements as you go is simply part of the job now.

Who is this for: Government employees, public service managers, civil servants, policy officers, non-profit staff, community organization workers, public administration professionals, local government officials, social workers, case managers
Team or Dept this is for: Government Affairs, Public Administration, Policy Development, Social Services, Human Services, Operations, Risk Management, Compliance, Non-Profit Management
